content.full_text:
Dear Mr. Holmer:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTION
F.q,rn Polrrrc,rr Pnacrrcrs C ounrtssroN
P.O. Box 807 ..128 J Street. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
April 12,2001
Kenneth R. Holmer, C.P.A.
Miller, Kaplan, Arase & Co., LLP
4 I 23 Lat*ershim B oulevard
North Hollywood, CA 91602
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-060
A local union PAC receives its contributions from membership dues, which are
transmitted by an affiliated district council PAC. Each membership contribution is less
than $100 per person per calendar year. May the local union PAC qualify as a small
contributor committee even though its membership contributions are passed through a
district council PAC?
CONCLUSION
Yes. The committee may qualify as a small contributor committee even if its
contributions pass through an intermediate unit of its sponsor.
FACTS
Your client is a labor organization district council PAC. The district council PAC
receives contributions from union members through membership dues. These dues are
then transmitted to affiliated local union PACs. No one union member contributes more
than $100 in a calendar year.
i Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 1 09- I 8996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-01-060
Page No. 2
The membership dues come from payroll deductions that the employer transmits
to the Industry Benefit Trust Fund along with medical, pension and other benefit
contributions. The trust fund then transmits the PAC contributions to the district council
PAC, which then transfers each member's individual contribution to his/her own local
union in which he/she is a member.
ANALYSIS
Under the Act, as amended by Proposition34, a small contributor committee is a
committee that meets all of the following requirements:
t The committee has been in existence for at least six months;
a The committee receives contributions from 100 or more persons on a
recurrent basis every 36 months and before contributing at the higher limit;
t The committee's campaign funds do not include any contributions exceeding
$200 per person per calendar year; and
a The committee makes contributions totaling $25 or more to five or more
candidates on a reculrent basis every 36 months. (Section 85203; Regulation
18s03.)
Your request concerns the third requirement that the committee's campaign funds
do not include any contributions exceeding $200 per person per calendar year.
(Regulation 18503(a)(3).) The specific issue raised by your request is whether a
committee can qualify as a small contributor committee if it receives its small
contributions through an intermediary. For purposes of this letter, we assume that a local
union PAC that wishes to qualify as a small contributor committee has met all of the
other criteria in Section 85203.
Proposition 34 did not amend the Act's rules on intermediaries. The Act provides
that one person might act as an intermediary by collecting a contribution for the benefit
of another person. (Section 84302; Reg. 18432.5.) The source of the resulting
contribution to the second person is the original contributor, not the intermediary person.
We have advised that a person will be considered an intermediary if at the time of the
contribution, the original contributor knowingly and unambiguously earmarked the
contribution to be deposited with the second person. (See, e.g., Miller Advice Letter, No.
A-99-254; Olson Advice Letter, No. I-97-321; Krausse Advice Letter, No. A-96-349.)
A payment is earmarked for political purposes when, at the time of making the
payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the funds with which the payment
will be commingled will be used to make contributions or expenditures. (Reg.
18215(b)(1).) Factors relevant for determining whether a donor has reason to know that
his or her payment will be used for political purposes can include the established practice
of the organization collecting the payment, and any representations made by the
organization when the payment is made. (See, e.g., McCray Advice Letter, No. A-89-
334.)


File No. A-01-060
Page No. 3
In the case of a sponsored committee, like a local union PAC, the source of a
member contribution received by the sponsored committee is the member and not the
sponsor or intermediate unit2 that channeled the contribution. A "member contribution"
is a payment made by a member of a sponsoring organization, who at the time of making
the payment knows or has reason to know that the payment will be used by the sponsored
committee to make contributions and expenditures. @eg. l8al9(a)(5).)
Applying these rules here, it would appear that the local union PACs may
attribute the contributions it receives to the union members rather than to the district
council PAC. A local union PAC may only do so, however, if the members know or
have reason to know that their payments will eventually be deposited with the local union
PAC. If this is the case, then the local union PAC may qualify as a small contributor
committee since no one member contributes more than $200 in a calendar year.
Please keep in mind that special reporting rules apply to contributions received
through intermediaries. Typically, a committee must itemize or report detailed
information about the intermediary of a contribution only if the cumulative amount of the
contribution from the original contributor equals $100 or more in a calendar year.
(Section 84302; Cathcart Advice Letter, No. A-00-101.) However, a sponsored
committee must report a sponsor acting as an intermediary of a lump sum payment of
$100 or more regardless of whether any one member contributed $100 or more. (Reg.
18a19(c)(3).) The sponsored committee must also report an intermediate unit acting as
an intermediary only if the name of the intermediate unit is zubstantially different from
the name of the sponsor. @eg. 18a19(f .)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
\<_
By:
Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:JB:jg
I:\Adviceltrs\01-060
2 "lntermediate unit" refers to a unit or similar component of a sponsor tlnt collects money from its
members and, at the time of the payment, the member knows or has reason to know that the payment will
be used by the sponsored committee to make contributions. (Reg. 18a19(c)(3).)
